concept.title: Concept Title
concept.shortdesc: This section will introduce you to good installation practices for Python and its libraries. You will learn about the difference between system-wide versus user-specific installations of Python and differences between package managers for Python.

conbody.p: The words package managers and virtual environments will come up in this section. For now, imagine a package manager as the local tool supplier and virtual environments as specific tool collections.
First, let's understand why you need a plan. Then we will explore the options.

section.title: Good Installation Practices
section.p:
The problem:
The version of Python matters. You don't necessarily need the latest version, but you the one that works best for all libraries used in the project.
For each programming project, you might need a unique combination of Python version and libraries versions.
Installing Python system-wide can collide with what other users want and cause problems for other software that relies on Python.

The solution:
Mind other users: Use user-specific installations of Python and libraries.
Efficient storage: Use a package manager to avoid redundant installations.
Prepare for different needs: Use virtual environments to manage different combinations of Python and library versions for your projects.

section.title: Difference between system-wide and user-specific installations
section.p:
Pros and cons of system-wide installations:
Pro: Efficiency. One single installation saves disk space if multiple people use the same computer.
Con: "Dependency Hell." If Project A needs Version 1 of a tool and Project B needs Version 2, a system-wide setup can only hold one, inevitably breaking the other project.
contentCon: System Risk. Many operating systems (like macOS and Linux) rely on an internal Python version to run basic tasks. Changing system-wide files can cause your entire computer to stop working correctly.
Con: Permissions. You often need "Administrator" or "Sudo" rights to make any changes, which is a major hurdle in restricted corporate environments.

Pros and cons of user-specific installations:
Pro: Safety. You don't need "Administrator" permissions to change things, and you won't accidentally break your computer's core functions.
Pro: Control. You can delete or "reset" your Python setup whenever things get messy without affecting any other user on the machine.
Con: Redundancy. You might end up with multiple versions of the same library, taking up slightly more hard drive space.

section.title: Different Package Managers
section.p: There are different package managers for Python, such as pip, uv and conda. Other programing software has other package managers; javascript has npm. Each has its own pros and cons.

Pros and cons of pip, conda and uv:
pip: The "standard" tool. It is lightweight and comes pre-installed with Python, but it only manages libraries, not the Python version itself.
Conda / Miniconda: The "all-in-one" choice. It is the gold standard for data science because it manages both your libraries AND the specific version of Python you need for each project.
uv: The "speedster." A modern tool that does everything pip does but is significantly faster and better at catching installation errors before they happen.

Why using Miniconda is a good choice for this non-devs in language processing:
It is beginner-friendly. Install Miniconda and get a complete Python environment with package management and virtual environment support.
Having the ability to install different Python versions is important for natural language processing libraries in Python.
Con: It can be resource-intensive, as it may require more disk space and memory compared to other lightweight options.

section.title: Summary
section.p: How you set up your Python environment depends on how you use your computer and on your level of experience. As a beginner, it's often recommended to use user-specific installations. Starting the installation with Miniconda is beginner-friendly because you get Python and a way to manage packages and virtual environments in one go.
